Wolf Eel (Anarrhichthys ocellatus) reaches 240 cm and requires cold North Pacific water (1–10°C) incompatible with typical tropical home aquariums; multiple sources explicitly categorize it as suitable for large public aquarium or zoo display only — a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this practicality warning to home aquarists researching the species. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-036 (Threadfin Lookdown).

Wolf Eel forms documented lifelong monogamous pair bonds — both sexes cohabit the same den for decades and cooperate in egg guarding; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface pair-bonding acquisition and housing guidance to aquarists. Fifth independent proposal after sw-fish-034, sw-fish-032, sw-fish-053, and sw-070.

Cubicus Boxfish (and all Ostraciidae) can release ostracitoxin from mucous glands when stressed or dying, rapidly killing all tankmates in a closed system — a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this critical safety warning in the UI, distinct from venomousSpines (handler-injected venom) and aggression. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-055 (Longhorn Cowfish) and sw-fish-069 (Arothron Puffer).

Cubicus Boxfish juveniles are vivid yellow with brown spots; adults fade to brownish coloration with yellow-spotted patterning — a change striking enough that juveniles and adults look like different species in vendor photos; a typed boolean would allow the UI to warn buyers about the transformation. Fifth independent proposal after sw-fish-048, sw-fish-056, sw-fish-085, and sw-fish-079.

Fijian Mimic Lemon Peel Tang, like other Acanthurus surgeonfish, is prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when dietary diversity or water quality is inadequate; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface a disease-risk warning and diet/water-quality guidance for HLLE-susceptible species. Fourth independent proposal after sw-fish-015 (Yellow Tang), sw-fish-066 (Sohal Tang), and sw-fish-076 (Black-Eared Tang).

Acanthurus pyroferus undergoes a dramatic ontogenetic color change: juveniles are vivid yellow with blue eye highlights (mimicking Centropyge flavissimus), while adults become tan/brown with red highlights and green-tinged fins — so pronounced that juveniles and adults are often not recognized as the same fish in the trade. Fourth independent proposal after sw-fish-047 (Jewel Damselfish), sw-fish-078 (Blackmouth Bicolor Chromis), and sw-fish-091 (Blue Velvet Damselfish).

Gymnothorax miliaris is documented by FishBase as occasionally hunting cooperatively with other species (e.g. groupers) — an unusual behavior for moray eels that would be useful to surface in a typed boolean for species-filter UI and keeper education.

Chrysurus Angelfish is documented to produce an audible clicking sound when stressed — a distinctive behavioral trait affecting keeper experience and stress-detection; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface vocalization-capable species in the UI. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-002 (Humu Rectangle Triggerfish) and sw-fish-045 (Jackknife Fish).

Apolemichthys trimaculatus forms small harems of one dominant male and 4–5 females in the wild (Monaco Nature Encyclopedia); a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface harem-keeping and protogynous hermaphroditism guidance to fishkeepers. Sixth independent proposal after sw-fish-005, sw-fish-049, sw-fish-039, sw-fish-093, and sw-fish-109.

Bluestripe Pipefish (Doryrhamphus excisus) use their prehensile tails to anchor onto gorgonians, coral branches, and macroalgae; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this non-obvious tank-setup requirement to fishkeepers distinctly from general decorPreferences. Fifth independent proposal after sw-fish-004 (Estuary Seahorse), sw-fish-053 (Lined Seahorse), sw-fish-044 (Ingen's Seahorse), and sw-fish-103 (Dragonface Pipefish).

Bluestripe Pipefish are slow feeders easily outcompeted by faster-moving fish; sources consistently advise housing in a species-only or pipefish/seahorse-only aquarium to prevent starvation — a typed boolean (analogous to crustacean.speciesOnlyTankRecommended) would surface this critical compatibility constraint. Sixth independent proposal after sw-fish-004, sw-fish-020, sw-fish-053, sw-fish-044, and sw-fish-103.

Fowleri Tang (Acanthurus fowleri), like other Acanthurus surgeonfish, is prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when water quality or dietary diversity is inadequate; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface a disease-risk warning and diet/water-quality guidance for HLLE-susceptible species. Fifth independent proposal after sw-fish-015 (Yellow Tang), sw-fish-066 (Sohal Tang), sw-fish-076 (Black-Eared Tang), and sw-fish-108 (Fijian Mimic Lemon Peel Tang).

Gemmatum Tang (Zebrasoma gemmatum), like other Acanthurus and Zebrasoma surgeonfish, is prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when dietary diversity or water quality is inadequate; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface a disease-risk warning and diet/water-quality guidance for HLLE-susceptible surgeonfish. Fifth independent proposal after sw-fish-015 (Yellow Tang), sw-fish-066 (Sohal Tang), sw-fish-076 (Black-Eared Tang), and sw-fish-108 (Fijian Mimic Lemon Peel Tang).

Eviota gobies (including E. pellucida) are documented to have exceptionally short natural lifespans of months to ~2 years; a typed boolean would help aquarists distinguish senescence from disease and set appropriate care expectations for these very short-lived species.

Coradion altivelis is described as seldom available in the aquarium trade; a typed enum (common/occasional/rare/very-rare) would help fishkeepers know whether to expect to source a species from a local fish store vs specialist importers.

Dischistodus prosopotaenia and several other damselfish species actively cultivate and defend algae patches within their territory (algae farming behavior); a typed boolean would surface this distinctive husbandry trait — these fish uproot corals and defend territory to grow algae gardens — to aquarists planning reef tanks.

Some fish (seahorses, pipefish, Discordipinna gobies) are so reliant on live microfauna that they often fail to acclimate to prepared foods; a typed enum (none/preferred/required) would let the UI warn fishkeepers before purchase and is more actionable than a careNotes sentence.

Heniochus acuminatus juveniles are documented acting as cleaner fish at cleaning stations, picking ectoparasites from other fish — a behaviorally significant trait relevant to tank setup and inter-species interactions; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this ecological role to fishkeepers. Fourth independent proposal after sw-fish-008 (French Angelfish), sw-fish-035 (Gray Angelfish), and sw-062 (Queen Angel).

Several species (High-Hat, mandarin dragonet, seahorses) are notorious for refusing prepared foods and requiring live prey — a typed enum (easy/moderate/difficult/live-only) would surface feeding difficulty separate from overall careLevel and help buyers plan acclimation.

Elacatinus evelynae and several other species (neon gobies, cleaner wrasses, cleaner shrimp) exhibit cleaning station behavior — removing parasites from other fish; a typed boolean would allow the UI to surface this ecologically important trait, help match compatible fish that seek cleaners, and distinguish obligate vs facultative cleaners in careNotes.

Helmet Cowfish (Tetrosomus gibbosus) and other Ostraciidae can release ostracitoxin from mucous glands when stressed or dying, rapidly killing all tankmates in a closed system — a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this critical safety warning in the UI, distinct from venomousSpines and aggression. Fourth independent proposal after sw-fish-055 (Longhorn Cowfish), sw-fish-069 (Arothron Puffer), and sw-fish-101 (Cubicus Boxfish).
Ecsenius lineatus and some other blennies lack true teeth; all food must be suitably small/soft — a boolean flag would surface this feeding constraint to fishkeepers without burying it in careNotes.

Island Cowfish (Acanthostracion notacanthus) and all Ostraciidae can release ostracitoxin (pahutoxin) from mucous glands when stressed or dying, rapidly poisoning all tankmates in a closed system — a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this critical safety warning in the UI, distinct from venomousSpines (handler-injected venom) and aggression. Fifth independent proposal after sw-fish-055 (Longhorn Cowfish), sw-fish-069 (Arothron Puffer), sw-fish-101 (Cubicus Boxfish), and sw-fish-117 (Helmet Cowfish).

Several shrimpgoby species (Cryptocentrus, Amblyeleotris, Stonogobiops genera) form obligate or facultative symbiotic partnerships with pistol shrimps; a typed boolean would let the UI surface this characteristic pairing behavior and suggest compatible shrimp species to buyers.

Soapfishes (Grammistidae) including Pogonoperca punctata produce grammistin, a toxic skin mucus released under stress or at death that can kill all other aquarium inhabitants; a typed boolean would allow the UI to display a prominent safety warning for these species.

Lunare Wrasse (and other Thalassoma wrasses) bury themselves in sand at night and when stressed; sources recommend a sand bed of at least 5 cm — a typed numeric field would surface this substrate requirement clearly in the UI, distinct from general decorPreferences. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel) and sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby).

Several hogfish and wrasse species (Bodianus spp., labrids) have juveniles that function as cleaner fish, removing parasites from other fish — a typed boolean would let the UI surface this behaviour and help aquarists plan a cleaning station.

Multiple sources specify distinct feeding frequencies for moray eels (twice weekly for juveniles, once weekly for adults); a typed enum field parallel to coral.feedingFrequency would make this machine-readable and surfaceable in the UI rather than buried in careNotes.

Arothron immaculatus and some other marine fish (certain puffers, archerfish, monos) tolerate a wide salinity range including brackish conditions; a boolean euryhaline flag would let the UI surface species suitable for brackish-to-marine transitional setups.

Doryrhamphus janssi is a well-documented adult cleaner fish that maintains cleaning stations on the reef, removing ectoparasites from cardinalfish and damsels; a typed boolean would surface this ecological role in the UI and help aquarists understand its value and behavioral needs (low-aggression tankmates, open swimming approach paths).

Calloplesiops altivelis has been reported to undergo sex change in captive all-male groups; a typed enum (protogynous/protandrous/bidirectional/none) would let the UI surface this husbandry-relevant trait — the existing fish.protogynous boolean cannot capture bidirectional or context-dependent sex change.

Longnose Batfish (and other batfish, anglerfish relatives, and seahorses) commonly refuse prepared foods and may require long-term live-food regimens; a typed enum (easy/moderate/difficult/live-only) would let the UI warn buyers before purchase and is more actionable than a careNotes sentence. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-120 (High-Hat).

Chaetodon larvatus feeds almost exclusively on Acropora coral polyps and rarely accepts substitute foods in captivity, making it essentially impossible to maintain long-term; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface an extreme diet-difficulty warning distinct from careLevel and reefSafe, helping buyers identify species with very low captive survival. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-021 (Eight-Banded Butterflyfish).

Pterois miles is an established invasive species in the Mediterranean Sea and Atlantic (including Caribbean); a typed array of region strings would let the UI flag ecologically sensitive species and inform hobbyists of invasive status and associated shipping restrictions (e.g. cannot ship to Florida). Parallel to the sw-fish-083 (Blue Dot Grouper) proposal.

Purple Tilefish (Hoplolatilus purpureus) is an obligate sand-burrowing species requiring a substrate of at least 7-8 cm of mixed sand, coral rubble, and broken shell for shelter construction; a typed numeric field on the tank block would surface this critical substrate requirement clearly in the UI distinct from general decorPreferences. Fourth independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel, 20 cm), sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby, 7 cm), and sw-030 (Lunare Wrasse, 5 cm).

Pinnatus Batfish (Platax pinnatus) is widely cited as expert-only specifically because newly imported wild-caught specimens routinely refuse prepared foods and waste away; the species's defining husbandry risk is feeding, not water-parameter sensitivity or aggression. A typed enum (easy/moderate/difficult/live-only) would let the UI surface this primary risk distinct from careLevel. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-120 (High-Hat) and sw-fish-137 (Longnose Batfish).

Fusiliers and anthias are described across sources as constant-grazing planktivores that require 4–6 small feedings per day to stay healthy; a typed boolean would let the UI surface the multi-daily feeding burden as a husbandry-cost flag distinct from diet.type.

Centropyge ferrugata is documented on FishBase as having bi-directional sex change confirmed, which the existing fish.protogynous boolean cannot accurately represent (true is technically correct but loses the bi-directional nuance). A typed enum (none/protogynous/protandrous/bidirectional) would more faithfully represent species like this and the marine betta (sw-fish-143, already proposed). Second independent vote for this field.

Pseudanthias rubrizonatus, like other anthias, is described across sources as a constant-grazing planktivore requiring 3-5 small feedings per day in captivity; individuals that go off food decline rapidly. A typed boolean would surface this multi-daily-feeding husbandry burden distinct from diet.type=planktivore. Second independent vote after sw-fish-172 (Redbelly Yellowtail Fusilier).

Rhinopias frondosa is widely reported across sources to refuse prepared foods and frequently require long-term live-feeder regimens (ghost shrimp, feeder shrimp, small fish); careLevel alone does not capture that the primary husbandry hurdle is feeding rather than water chemistry. A typed enum (easy/moderate/difficult/live-only) would surface this risk pre-purchase. Fourth independent vote after sw-fish-120 (High-Hat), sw-fish-137 (Longnose Batfish), and sw-fish-155 (Pinnatus Batfish).

Rhinopias spp. (and Taenianotus triacanthus, sw-094) periodically shed their outer cuticle layer, presenting cloudy eyes and visible peeling that mimic disease and alarm new keepers; a typed boolean would let the UI distinguish this normal husbandry trait from pathology. Second independent observation after sw-094 (Leaf Scorpionfish).

Fang blennies (Meiacanthus spp.) deliver venom via grooved lower-jaw canines, not via fin spines, so using the existing fish.venomousSpines boolean conflates two anatomically distinct envenomation modes (spine-mediated defense like rabbitfish/lionfish vs bite-mediated defense like fang blennies). A separate fish.venomousFangs boolean would let the UI surface the more relevant safety guidance — avoid hand-feeding — without misleading aquarists into thinking fin handling is the hazard. Independent vote following sw-fish-026 (Forktail Blenny) and sw-fish-196 (Smith's Blenny), both of which used venomousSpines as the closest-available proxy for fang venom.

Spotted/Harlequin Sweetlips (Plectorhinchus chaetodonoides) is widely reported across LiveAquaria and aquarium-hobby sources to refuse prepared foods on arrival and require live saltwater feeder shrimp (or freshwater ghost shrimp) to initiate feeding, with starvation a documented failure mode in captivity; careLevel alone does not capture that the primary husbandry hurdle is feeding rather than water chemistry. A typed enum (easy/moderate/difficult/live-only) would surface this pre-purchase. Fifth independent vote after sw-fish-120 (High-Hat), sw-fish-137 (Longnose Batfish), sw-fish-155 (Pinnatus Batfish), and sw-fish-176 (Rhinopias Scorpionfish).

Amblyeleotris steinitzi is an obligate burrow partner with alpheid pistol shrimp (typically Alpheus djeddensis); the partnership is so defining of the species that it shapes tank setup, compatible-tankmate choice, and substrate requirements. A typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface this characteristic shrimpgoby pairing behavior and suggest compatible Alpheus species to buyers. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-130 (Lagoon Shrimp Goby).

Liopropoma rubre is described in field guides as "common but secretive and rarely seen" and in aquarium settings spends the majority of its time tucked inside caves and overhangs, only briefly emerging to feed. This cryptic/cave-dwelling lifestyle is shared by other reef species (other Liopropoma basslets, dottybacks, scorpionfish, jawfish, moray eels, etc.) and is husbandry-relevant — aquarists need to plan for extensive rockwork and accept that the fish will rarely be visible. A typed boolean would let the UI warn display-tank buyers that they may see this species infrequently, distinct from temperament or activity-zone.

Paraplesiops meleagris and other southern-Australia / temperate-reef species require cool water (~15–22°C) and typically a chiller in domestic climates; this is a major husbandry distinction from tropical marine fish that affects equipment selection and tankmate filtering. A typed top-level boolean (or shared enum across taxa) would let the UI flag temperate-only systems pre-purchase.

Siganus guttatus is documented as tolerating (and juveniles preferring) low-salinity inshore, mangrove, and estuarine habitats; a boolean euryhaline flag on the fish variant would let the UI surface species suitable for brackish-to-marine transitional setups. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-125 (Immaculatus Puffer).

Gymnothorax nudivomer secretes a toxic mucus that is documented as both defensive (handler-risk) and a deterrent to consumption — distinct from venomousSpines (no injected venom) and from toxicToTank (the toxin is on the skin, not a stress/death release that kills tank inhabitants). Other Gymnothorax species and certain pufferfish/boxfish/soapfish share a skin-toxin trait that affects glove/handling guidance specifically rather than tank survivability. A typed boolean would let the UI surface handling warnings independently of the existing tank-kill toxin and venomous-spine fields.

Mulloidichthys martinicus is documented to form day-resting schools alongside smallmouth grunts (Haemulon chrysargyreum) as social protective mimicry — a recurring trait across some snappers, juvenile goatfishes, and mimic blennies that currently has no typed slot and falls into careNotes prose. A typed boolean would let the UI surface heterospecific-school behavior to fishkeepers planning multi-species displays.

Many Halichoeres wrasses, other sand wrasses, jawfish, and certain gobies require a deep fine sand bed (typically >=5 cm) to burrow into for sleeping and refuge — a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this non-obvious substrate requirement distinct from the array-typed decorPreferences and is critical husbandry info.

Diademichthys lineatus is an obligate commensal of long-spine sea urchins (Diadema spp.) and branching SPS corals — both wild ecology and captive husbandry depend on providing a host structure for shelter and as a foraging substrate. This shares structure with shrimpgoby/pistol-shrimp pairings (already proposed as fish.symbioticShrimpPartner by sw-fish-130 and sw-fish-208) and with seahorse hitching-post needs (already typed as fish.requiresHitchingPost). A general fish.requiresHostSymbiont boolean (or per-host enum) would consolidate these obligate-relationship cases under one typed field so the UI can warn buyers pre-purchase that the species needs a living co-tenant rather than just decor.

Black Neon Dottyback is a captive-bred designer hybrid (Pseudochromis springeri x P. aldabraensis) with no formal binomial; a typed boolean (already proposed for Blood Parrot Cichlid) would let the UI flag hybrid origin and explain the non-standard scientificName format.

Cryptocentrus pavoninoides is one of the classic shrimp gobies that forms a burrow-sharing symbiosis with pistol shrimp (Alpheus spp.); the relationship is defining of the species' tank-setup needs (deep sand, rubble, compatible Alpheus partner). Third independent vote after sw-fish-130 (Lagoon Shrimp Goby) and sw-fish-208 (Steinitz Goby) — a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface shrimpgoby pairing behavior and recommend compatible shrimp species pre-purchase.

Gobiodon okinawae and several other coral-dwelling gobies undergo bidirectional sex change (either sex can switch as needed to form a pair), which is distinct from the standard protogynous/protandrous flags and affects pair-acquisition guidance (any two individuals can pair).

Amblyeleotris yanoi forms an obligate burrow-sharing symbiosis with Alpheus randalli (Randall's pistol shrimp); this is defining for the species' tank-setup needs (deep fine sand, rubble, compatible Alpheus partner sold/added together). Fourth independent vote after sw-fish-130 (Lagoon Shrimp Goby), sw-fish-208 (Steinitz Goby), and sw-fish-305 (Bluespotted Watchman Goby) — a typed boolean would let the UI surface shrimpgoby pairing pre-purchase.

Amblyeleotris yanoi males reach ~13 cm SL while females reach only ~5-6 cm SL — a pair will look noticeably mismatched and the single adultSizeCm range understates the spread. Other shrimp gobies, anglerfishes, and many wrasses also show pronounced size dimorphism; a boolean (with detail in careNotes) would let the UI surface that the reported max applies to one sex only.

Pseudochromis aldabraensis is documented on FishBase as having bi-directional sex change confirmed — the existing fish.protogynous boolean cannot represent bidirectional (true is technically incorrect for bidirectional species). A typed enum (none/protogynous/protandrous/bidirectional) would more faithfully represent dottybacks and the prior proposals from sw-fish-143 (Marine Betta) and sw-fish-178 (Rusty Angelfish). Third independent vote for this field.

Deepwater species such as Neptune Grouper (collected from 80–250 m), some anglers, and certain basslets are reported to be stressed by typical high-intensity reef lighting and need shaded/dim setups; multiple specialist sources flag this as a husbandry-critical trait with no current typed slot — it currently falls into careNotes prose. A typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface lighting-setup guidance pre-purchase.

Enoplosus armatus is a temperate southern-Australian reef fish requiring cool water (~18–24°C, FishBase preferred 15.3–20.6°C); like other temperate marine species this is a major husbandry distinction from tropical fish that affects equipment selection (chiller) and tankmate filtering. Seconding the prior suggestion from sw-fish-240 (Western Blue Devil) — a typed boolean would let the UI flag temperate-only systems pre-purchase.

Pseudochromis flavivertex is documented on FishBase as having bi-directional sex change confirmed — the existing fish.protogynous boolean cannot represent bidirectional (true is technically incorrect for bidirectional species, and hobby sources for this species inconsistently call it 'protogynous' while FishBase calls it 'bi-directional'). A typed enum (none/protogynous/protandrous/bidirectional) would more faithfully represent dottybacks and other bidirectional species. Fourth independent vote for this field after sw-fish-143 (Marine Betta), sw-fish-178 (Rusty Angelfish), and sw-fish-367 (Neon Dottyback).

Chromis vanderbilti and related schooling chromis/damsels are well-documented to suffer progressive conspecific attrition in captivity — dominant individuals harass subordinates until only one or two remain from an initial group. A typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI warn buyers that groups bought as a school may not remain a school, and prompt guidance to start with larger groups or a roomy tank.

Waitei Anthias, like other anthias and fusilier planktivores, requires 3-5 small feedings per day and declines rapidly if underfed; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this multi-daily-feeding husbandry burden to aquarists distinct from diet.type=planktivore. Third independent vote after sw-fish-172 (Redbelly Yellowtail Fusilier) and sw-fish-166 (Red-Belted Anthias).

Arothron nigropunctatus exists in at least three stable adult color phases (grey-brown with black spots, half-yellow, and all-yellow) that are intraspecific variants present in adults, not a juvenile-to-adult transition — the existing fish.juvenileColorPhase boolean does not capture adult-phase polymorphism where any adult individual may appear in a markedly different color form. Several other Arothron and Tetraodontidae species share this trait. A typed boolean would let the UI warn buyers that specimens sold under the same name may look very different, preventing confusion with disease or stress.

Cirrhilabrus flavidorsalis males display dynamic color changes across the entire body (dorsal fin from yellow to red or blue; body from red, pink, or white) in response to mood, social context, and spawning readiness — distinct from fish.juvenileColorPhase (which is ontogenetic). A typed boolean would let the UI surface this trait as a visual-interest talking point and help buyers understand that color variability is normal and not a disease sign.

Gymnothorax meleagris (and moray eels generally) have powerful jaws, sharp teeth, and poor eyesight that make accidental handler bites a well-documented risk — distinct from venomousSpines (no venom) and conspecificAggression (about tankmate behavior); a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface a handler-safety warning and reinforce no-hand-feeding guidance for moray eels and other bite-risk species. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-105 (Dwarf Moray Eel).

Gymnothorax fimbriatus and other large moray eels are prominent bite hazards due to powerful jaws, sharp teeth, and poor eyesight — bites cause serious bacterial infections requiring prompt medical attention; a typed boolean would surface a handler-safety warning distinct from venomousSpines (no injected venom) and separate from conspecificAggression (which is about tankmate behavior, not human handling). Second independent proposal for this field after sw-fish-105 (Dwarf Moray Eel).

Hawkfish (Cirrhitidae) and other ambush-perch predators (scorpionfish, certain gobies) spend the vast majority of their time motionless on a chosen rock or coral head, lunging only when prey passes close — they do not swim actively. A typed boolean would let the UI surface tank-setup guidance (provide prominent perch rocks) and help fishkeepers understand why the fish appears inactive, distinct from swimZone (which describes vertical position rather than locomotion style).

Yellow Priolepis Goby and other cave/crevice-dwelling gobies spend most of their time hidden in rockwork and are rarely seen in the open — a typed boolean would let the UI warn buyers that this species has very low visibility in typical aquarium settings, distinct from nocturnal behavior.

Frogfish, scorpionfish, and similar sit-and-wait predators pose a tankmate-size risk independent of temperament — they are generally peaceful but will engulf prey up to their own body length; a typed boolean would let the UI surface a size-based incompatibility warning separate from temperament or speciesOnlyTankRecommended.

Pycnochromis acares is a schooling chromis that, like other Chromis and Pycnochromis species, tends to suffer progressive conspecific attrition in captivity where dominant individuals harass subordinates until only one or two remain from the initial group; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI warn buyers that school-purchased groups may dwindle and prompt guidance to start with larger groups in roomy tanks. Second independent vote after sw-fish-428 (Vanderbilt Chromis).

Pycnochromis acares is a benthopelagic planktivore that in the wild continuously feeds on passing plankton throughout the day; captive specimens have been known to decline without multiple small daily feedings. A typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this multi-feeding husbandry burden to aquarists, distinct from diet.type=planktivore. Fourth independent vote for this field after sw-fish-172, sw-fish-166, and sw-fish-429.

Amblyeleotris wheeleri forms a facultative-to-obligate burrow-sharing symbiosis with Alpheus ochrostriatus (and related pistol shrimps); providing a compatible Alpheus partner is commonly advised for the species' behavioral wellbeing and strongly shapes tank-setup requirements (deep sand, rubble, calm flow). Fifth independent vote after sw-fish-130 (Lagoon Shrimp Goby), sw-fish-208 (Steinitz Goby), sw-fish-305 (Bluespotted Watchman Goby), and sw-fish-337 (Flagtail Shrimp Goby) — this field now meets the 5-vote promotion threshold.

Wheeler's Shrimp Goby (and all Amblyeleotris/Cryptocentrus/Stonogobiops shrimp gobies) require a minimum fine sand bed depth of 5-8 cm for burrow construction by the partner pistol shrimp; a typed numeric field would surface this substrate requirement clearly in the UI, distinct from general decorPreferences and already proposed by sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel, 20 cm), sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby, 7 cm), sw-030 (Lunare Wrasse, 5 cm), and sw-fish-162 (Purple Tilefish, 8 cm).

FishBase notes Pomacanthus annularis is only infrequently exported for the aquarium trade, and several other species in the catalogue have similar rarity caveats (deep-water species, restricted-range endemic species); a typed enum (common/occasional/rare/uncommon) on the fish variant would let the UI surface trade-availability context and help buyers manage expectations about sourcing difficulty.

Cirrhilabrus cyanopleura (Blue Sided Fairy Wrasse) and Cirrhilabrus ryukyuensis (Ryukyu Wrasse) are two distinct species both sold under the trade name "Yellow-Flanked Fairy Wrasse" — a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI flag species prone to trade-name confusion and surface a buyer-verification note to check the scientific name before purchase.

Yellow Candy Hogfish and several other deep-collected reef species (e.g. deep-water anthias, some gobies) are collected from 30–100 m depth; a typed nullableNumericRange for typical collection depth would let the UI flag species prone to decompression stress in transit and surface temperature/lighting guidance informed by their natural light environment.

Bothus lunatus (Peacock Flounder) buries itself in sand to camouflage and ambush prey; sources note fine sandy substrate is essential. A typed numeric field for minimum sand depth would surface this requirement clearly in the UI distinct from general decorPreferences — sixth independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel, 20 cm), sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby, 7 cm), sw-030 (Lunare Wrasse, 5 cm), sw-fish-162 (Purple Tilefish, 8 cm), and sw-fish-430 (Wheeler's Shrimp Goby, 5 cm).

Bothus lunatus and other flatfish, scorpionfish, and anglerfish are obligate ambush predators that rely on camouflage and stillness to capture prey — a typed boolean would surface feeding-behavior context (will not actively chase food) and flag that target-feeding during acclimation may be required; parallel to fish.perchHunter proposed for hawkfish (sw-fish-427) and fish.ambushPredator already proposed for wartskin angler (sw-fish-444).

Sufflamen chrysopterum and other triggerfish (Balistidae) are documented to produce audible grunting/croaking sounds via the swimbladder — a distinctive behavioral trait that affects keeper experience and tank-room noise; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface species capable of vocalization in the UI. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-002 (Humu Rectangle Triggerfish) and sw-fish-045 (Jackknife Fish).

Ostorhinchus nigrofasciatus was formerly Apogon nigrofasciatus; a boolean flag (or a synonymousScientificName string field) would help surface authoritative genus reclassifications to prevent keepers and retailers from using outdated binomials, which is a recurring pattern across the Apogonidae family.

Australian Stripey and several other naturally schooling fish (chromis, cardinalfish, anthias) benefit strongly from being kept in groups of 3+ — a typed boolean would let the UI surface a "keep in groups" warning distinct from the grouping enum which already covers shoaling/schooling but does not flag species for which solo keeping causes visible stress.

Burgess Butterflyfish and other deep-reef species (found at 40–130 m) prefer dimmer lighting and cover in captivity; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface low-light tank-setup warnings and deep-water habitat context to fishkeepers, distinct from the nocturnal field.

Black Bar Chromis and several other schooling chromis/damselfish become noticeably more aggressive when kept alone or in very small groups; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface a behavioral warning to buyers that isolation alters the species temperament, distinct from the numeric compatibility.minGroupSize field which does not flag the aggression consequence.

Odontanthias borbonius and other mesophotic/twilight-zone species (typically collected below 60 m) have direct husbandry implications — decompression stress during collection, preference for dimmer lighting, heightened acclimation difficulty, and higher retail prices; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface a mesophotic-origin warning and guide buyers toward appropriate acclimation and lighting protocols.

Strictly crevice-dwelling and nocturnal species like Priolepis nocturna are nearly impossible to observe feeding during daylight; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface a husbandry warning that keepers need to specifically verify feeding success for cryptic/cave-dwelling species, distinct from careLevel or feedingDifficulty.

Omobranchus anolius (Brachiosaurus Blenny) is documented as tolerant of both marine and brackish conditions (FishBase); a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI flag species suitable for lower-salinity or estuarine systems, helping aquarists identify candidates for subtidal estuary biotope tanks without having to set waterType to brackish.

Carberryi Anthias (and anthias generally) are active planktivores requiring 3-5 small feedings per day in captivity; individuals underfed rapidly decline. A typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this multi-daily-feeding husbandry burden to aquarists distinct from diet.type=planktivore. Fifth independent vote after sw-fish-172 (Redbelly Yellowtail Fusilier), sw-fish-166 (Red-Belted Anthias), sw-fish-429 (Waitei Anthias), and sw-fish-439 (Acares Midget Reef Chromis) — this field now meets the 5-vote promotion threshold.

Black & White Chromis (Pycnochromis margaritifer) is a conspecifically semi-aggressive schooling chromis that, like Pycnochromis acares and Chromis vanderbilti, is prone to progressive group attrition in captivity — dominant individuals harass subordinates until only one or two remain; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI warn buyers that school-purchased groups may dwindle and prompt guidance to start large groups in roomy tanks. Third independent vote for this field after sw-fish-428 (Vanderbilt Chromis) and sw-fish-439 (Acares Midget Reef Chromis).

Ogilbyina novaehollandiae shows pronounced sexual dichromatism — males are grayish with reddish heads while females range from pinkish to yellow or greenish; several other dottybacks, wrasses, and anthias share this trait. A typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface a coloration-variation warning to buyers who may be confused by the different appearances of males vs females in the trade.

FishBase notes isolated reports of ciguatera poisoning associated with Chaetodon auriga in the wild; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface a food-safety and wild-harvest warning to hobbyists researching this species — third independent proposal after sw-fish-022 (Emperor Red Snapper) and sw-fish-099 (Bluestripe Snapper).

Fang blennies (Meiacanthus spp.) deliver venom via specialized canine teeth in the lower jaw — a mechanism entirely distinct from venomousSpines (dorsal/anal fin spines). The current venomousSpines field does not cover fang-delivered venom; a typed boolean fish.venomousBite would surface a handler-safety and tankmate-injury warning specific to fang blennies and other jaw-envenoming species.

Serranus tortugarum and other Serranus spp. (e.g. Harlequin Bass) are synchronous (simultaneous) hermaphrodites — each individual possesses both functional male and female reproductive organs simultaneously; a typed boolean would surface this unusual reproductive biology distinct from protogynous/protandrous sequential hermaphroditism and help pair-keepers understand that any two individuals can spawn.

Moray eels (and a few other species) are a documented bite hazard during maintenance and feeding due to powerful jaws, sharp teeth, and poor eyesight — distinct from venomousSpines (which is a handling-injury from spines, not teeth) and from conspecificAggression (which covers tankmate behavior). A typed boolean would surface a handler-safety warning to aquarists who perform in-tank maintenance or hand-feed. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-105 (Dwarf Moray Eel).

Caribbean Redspotted Hawkfish and other hawkfish (Cirrhitidae) are pelagic spawners that scatter eggs into the water column — a trait relevant to captive breeding feasibility and distinct from substrate-spawning or mouthbrooding behaviors currently captured by breedingDifficulty; a typed boolean would make spawning mode machine-readable and surface context for the breedingNotes field.

FishBase describes Enchelycore bayeri as a rare species in the wild; some species are almost never seen in the aquarium trade while others are regularly available — a typed enum (common/occasional/rare/wild-caught-only) on the fish/fauna record would let the UI surface sourcing difficulty and help buyers set realistic expectations when searching for rarely traded species.

For rays and skates, adultSizeCm is ambiguous: scientific literature uses total length (TL) while aquarium husbandry conventionally uses disc width (DW). Urotrygon chilensis sources cite 41.9 cm TL (FishBase) vs. '16 inches' without specifying measurement type (LiveAquaria); a typed enum or note field (e.g., 'TL'/'DW') on the fauna record would let the UI display the correct measurement label and avoid misleading users comparing a ray's total length against a teleost's body length.

Canary Deep Water Damsel (Chrysiptera galba) is reportedly collected from depths of 45–60 m, well beyond typical shallow reef collection depths; for 'deep water' damsels and similar mesophotic species the collection depth affects decompression stress during transport, acclimation difficulty, and recommended lighting conditions. A typed nullableNumericRange (min/max, meters) on nativeRange would let the UI surface deep-collection sourcing warnings distinct from the general habitat depth range already captured as prose. Second independent proposal for a collection-depth field after sw-fish-433 (Yellow Candy Hogfish) proposed nativeRange.typicalDepthM.

Canthigaster bennetti is documented as predominantly herbivorous (FishBase trophic level 2.5, diet primarily filamentous green algae) unlike the carnivore-dominant feeding behavior typical of larger puffer genera; a typed boolean would help the UI surface algae-biased diet guidance and distinguish herbivorous tobies from hard-prey-focused puffers — the coarse diet.type enum (omnivore) does not capture this nuance.

Paraplesiops poweri is marketed as a 'grouper' in the aquarium trade but belongs to Plesiopidae (roundheads), not Serranidae (true groupers); a typed boolean would let the UI flag species whose popular trade name implies a different family, preventing buyers from expecting grouper-typical size, behavior, or care requirements.

Barnacle blennies (Acanthemblemaria spp.) and similar tube blennies occupy permanent small tubes or barnacle shells as territories; this trait affects decor requirements (hollow tubes, barnacle clusters), conspecific territorial density, and feeding approach (planktivore water-column feeding despite being a benthic fish) — distinct from general rockwork preferences captured in tank.decorPreferences and not expressible via any current enum.

Amblyeleotris randalli is one of the classic shrimpgoby species forming a burrow-sharing symbiosis with pistol shrimp (Alpheus ochrostriatus); the relationship is defining of the species' tank-setup needs (deep sand, rubble, a compatible Alpheus partner). Sixth independent vote for fish.symbioticShrimpPartner — field already meets the 5-vote promotion threshold from prior proposals (sw-fish-130, sw-fish-208, sw-fish-305, sw-fish-337, sw-fish-430).

Orange Stripe Prawn Goby shares a burrow with a pistol shrimp that excavates and maintains the burrow; a minimum sand bed depth of 5–7 cm is commonly recommended for shrimp gobies to support burrow construction. Seventh independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel, 20 cm), sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby, 7 cm), sw-030 (Lunare Wrasse, 5 cm), sw-fish-162 (Purple Tilefish, 8 cm), sw-fish-430 (Wheeler's Shrimp Goby, 5 cm), and sw-fish-443 (Assorted Flounder, 5 cm).

Pacific Redstripe Hogfish and other Bodianus hogfish actively forage by rooting through the substrate for small crustaceans and invertebrates, which can cause notable cloudiness in the water column; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface substrate-disturbance warnings (sediment suspension, cloudiness, potential sand-bed disruption for other bottom-dwellers) distinct from general decorPreferences.

Ecsenius blennies and other herbivorous grazer species actively consume microalgae from rock surfaces and are commonly recommended as part of a reef tank clean-up crew; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface algae-grazing suitability as a filtration/maintenance benefit, distinct from the coarse diet.type herbivore enum which does not distinguish active rock-grazers from open-water algae feeders.

Colin's Angelfish inhabits 20–110 m depths in the wild; a typed nullableNumericRange for natural depth range would help surface species with deepwater origins that may have specialized captive temperature and lighting needs distinct from shallow-reef species — the temperature disagreement between LiveAquaria and FishBase for this species is directly attributable to its deepwater natural habitat.

Pervagor melanocephalus inhabits clear coastal reefs and outer reef habitats at 20–40 m depth (FishBase); species with deepwater wild origins may require different acclimation, cooler temperature targets, and dimmer lighting than shallow-reef counterparts — a typed nullableNumericRange for natural depth range on the fish variant would let the UI surface deepwater-origin context. Third independent proposal for depth-range tracking after sw-fish-460 (nativeRange.collectionDepthM) and sw-fish-466 (fish.naturalDepthRangeM).

Coral Catfish (and several other species) exhibit fundamentally different grouping behavior as juveniles vs adults — juveniles form obligate schools of 100+ while adults become solitary or form small loose groups; the static grouping field cannot represent this life-stage shift, and a typed boolean would let the UI warn buyers that a schooling purchase requirement diminishes over time.

Several shrimp gobies (Stonogobiops spp., Amblyeleotris spp., Cryptocentrus spp.) maintain documented obligate-or-near-obligate symbiotic partnerships with specific pistol shrimp (Alpheus spp.) — a typed boolean would let the UI surface co-housing guidance and identify species where adding the partner shrimp is strongly advised rather than optional.

Acanthochromis polyacanthus is one of the very few marine fish that entirely lacks a pelagic larval stage — juveniles hatch as miniature adults from guarded benthic nests rather than dispersing into the plankton; this trait is a major driver of captive-breeding feasibility (no need for rearing planktonic larvae) and is not expressible via breedingDifficulty or any existing typed field.

Cephalopholis miniata (Coral Hind) and other large reef predatory groupers in the Indo-Pacific are documented ciguatera fish poisoning vectors; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface a food-safety and wild-harvest warning to hobbyists researching large predatory groupers — third independent proposal after sw-fish-022 (Emperor Red Snapper) and sw-fish-099 (Bluestripe Snapper).

Shrimp gobies (Amblyeleotris, Stonogobiops, Cryptocentrus spp.) form well-documented obligate-to-facultative symbioses with alpheid pistol shrimp, sharing a burrow — a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface guidance on acquiring a shrimp partner and setting up appropriate substrate, distinct from general compatibility fields.

Chaetodon mitratus is a deepwater species found at 22–80 m along steep outer reef drop-offs — a depth range that exceeds the typical shallow-reef aquarium collection zone and has direct husbandry implications (dimmer lighting preferred, decompression stress in transit, acclimation difficulty). Several other deep-collected marine fish have similar depth-stratified distributions. A typed nullableNumericRange field on nativeRange would let the UI flag deep-water species pre-purchase and surface lighting/acclimation guidance.

Mirror Butterflyfish (and several other Chaetodon species) are obligate corallivores that cannot be practically weaned onto prepared foods in captivity; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface an extreme diet-difficulty and viability warning beyond what feedingDifficulty='difficult' conveys, helping buyers identify species with very poor long-term captive survival without access to live coral. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-021 (Eight-Banded Butterflyfish).

Chaetodon declivis is endemic to only two island groups (Marquesas Islands and Line Islands in the Central Pacific), making it very rarely encountered in the aquarium trade; a typed boolean for restricted-range endemic status would let the UI surface sourcing-difficulty warnings and help buyers understand that wild-caught specimens may be infrequently available, distinct from the coarse tradeAvailability string already proposed by sw-fish-119, sw-fish-441, and sw-fish-448.

Siganus unimaculatus adults are documented forming permanent pairs including same-sex pairs (Wikipedia); this nuance is not captured by the existing monogamousPairing boolean (which implies an opposite-sex mate bond) — a separate typed boolean would let the UI surface that this species can be kept in same-sex pairs and does not require an opposite-sex partner for stable social behaviour.

Arothron manilensis is documented by FishBase as tolerating both marine and brackish waters in the wild (inhabiting estuaries, mangrove areas, and sheltered coastal shallows); a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI flag this species as suitable for lower-salinity or estuarine systems, helping aquarists identify candidates for brackish-to-marine transitional biotopes. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-125 (Immaculatus Puffer) and sw-fish-250 (Yellow Blotch Rabbitfish).

Siganus puellus juveniles form large schools around Acropora coral in shallow lagoons before transitioning to adult pair behavior — this ontogenetic shift in social structure (schooling juvenile vs. paired adult) is a distinct husbandry-relevant trait not captured by compatibility.grouping alone; a typed boolean would let the UI flag that juveniles may be sold in groups but adults prefer solitary/pair housing.

Anthias and other stress-prone reef fish sometimes revert to live-food-only feeding when newly introduced; a typed acclimationDifficulty enum (easy/moderate/difficult) distinct from feedingDifficulty would help surface species that require special quarantine/acclimation protocols beyond routine feeding difficulty.

Chaenopsis alepidota is documented as inhabiting empty worm tubes in sandy reef rubble, a defining behavioral trait that drives specific decor requirements (hollow tubes, PVC pipe, appropriately sized burrow entrances) and territorial dynamics not captured by tank.decorPreferences prose alone; typed boolean would let the UI surface tube/burrow-setup guidance for chaenopsid blennies and other worm-tube occupants. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-474 (Mexican Barnacle Blenny).

Cirrhilabrus naokoae is listed as Vulnerable (VU) by the IUCN (assessed 2023); several species researched in this project carry IUCN assessments that are currently buried in careNotes with no typed field — a typed nullable string enum (e.g. LC/NT/VU/EN/CR/EW/EX/DD/NE) would let the UI surface conservation-status warnings to buyers and researchers.

Apogonichthyoides melas (and cardinalfish family Apogonidae generally) are paternal mouthbrooders — males incubate egg masses orally until hatching; a typed boolean on the fish variant would make this reproductively-significant trait machine-readable and surfaceable in breeding filter UIs. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-012 (Kaudern s Cardinal / Banggai Cardinalfish).

Centropyge woodheadi is regularly confused with the closely related C. heraldi (Yellow Angelfish) in the aquarium trade — the two species are near-identical in coloration (both yellow), differ only by a black dorsal spot in woodheadi, and have overlapping ranges; a typed nullable string (scientific name of the commonly confused congener) on the fish variant would let the UI surface a buyer-awareness warning and help aquarists verify what they receive matches the advertised species.

Spotted Coral Croucher (and Caracanthus spp.) are documented to deposit eggs directly onto living SPS coral tissue (Pocillopora, Acropora) in captivity, causing localized tissue recession around the clutch — a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface a compatibility warning for SPS reef keepers beyond what reefSafe covers.

Many damselfish (Pomacentridae), including the Sulphur Damselfish, actively cultivate and defend a patch of turf algae as their primary territory — they weed out undesired algae species, bite coral polyps to create bare substrate, and aggressively drive off grazers; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface this specific coral-damaging behavior to reef keepers, distinct from general reefSafe or conspecificAggression.

Multiple shrimp goby species (Ctenogobiops tangaroai, Stonogobiops spp., Amblyeleotris spp., Cryptocentrus spp.) form obligate or facultative symbiotic burrow partnerships with alpheid (pistol) shrimp; a typed boolean would let the UI surface this behavior and prompt fishkeepers to consider pairing with a compatible pistol shrimp.

Liopropoma carmabi and other species collected from depths of 15+ m have meaningfully different lighting, temperature, and acclimation needs from shallow-reef congeners; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface reduced-lighting, cooler-temperature, and slow-acclimation guidance for deepwater-collected specimens, distinct from careLevel.

Pseudanthias lunulatus is collected from 40–75 m outer reef depths; deep-water-origin species require subdued lighting during acclimation, experience more decompression stress during collection, and have higher acclimation difficulty than shallow-reef equivalents — a typed boolean would let the UI surface mesophotic-origin warnings and guide buyers toward appropriate lighting and acclimation protocols. Fifth independent proposal (after sw-fish-455, sw-fish-459, sw-fish-493, and deepwater-candy-basslet).

Pristiapogon kallopterus, like all Apogonidae, is a paternal mouthbrooder (male incubates egg masses orally until hatching); a typed boolean on the fish variant would make this reproductively-significant trait machine-readable and surfaceable in breeding filter UIs. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-012 (Kaudern s Cardinal) and sw-fish-503 (Black Cardinalfish).

Cirrhilabrus isosceles and several other fairy wrasses are collected at depths of 24–60 m where barotrauma during ascent is a documented mortality risk; a typed boolean would let the UI flag species with elevated post-collection mortality and prompt buyers to seek aquaculture-raised or responsibly-vented specimens.

Hawkfishes (Cirrhitidae) use thickened lower pectoral fin rays to anchor on rocks and corals and ambush prey — this perch-and-wait locomotion style affects tank-layout needs (rockwork perching spots) and is distinct from swimZone or decorPreferences; a typed boolean would surface this behavioral trait to fishkeepers across all cirrhitid species.

Bella Goby (and other Valenciennea spp.) are obligate sand-sifters requiring a specific minimum sand-bed depth (5–10 cm) for burrowing and feeding behavior; a typed numeric field would surface this substrate requirement clearly in the UI distinct from general decorPreferences. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel, 20 cm) and sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby, 7 cm).

Multiple unrelated species share common trade names (e.g. both Pseudanthias flavicauda and Liopropoma mitratum are sold as Yellowtail Reef Basslet); a boolean flag or disambiguation note field would help the UI warn buyers to confirm scientific name before purchase.

Black Cap Jawfish requires a minimum sand bed of ~8 cm blended with rubble for burrow construction; a typed numeric field would surface this substrate depth requirement clearly in the UI, distinct from general decorPreferences. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel) and sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby).

The 'Assorted Angler' and similar 'Assorted X' trade listings (LiveAquaria, SaltwaterFish.com, etc.) cover multiple species at genus level rather than a single species; a typed boolean on the top-level species record (or a nullable string 'scientificNameNote') would let the UI warn buyers that the actual species they receive may vary, affecting adult size, venom presence, and care requirements — distinct from alsoKnownAs trade-name synonyms which imply a single species.

Broadstripe Goby (and several other Elacatinus/Gobiosoma species, plus several wrasses) function as cleaner fish at cleaning stations, removing ectoparasites from host fish; a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface this ecological role and help aquarists understand the species' reef role and inter-species behavior.

Elacatinus prochilos is listed as Vulnerable (VU) on the IUCN Red List; for several saltwater fish with threatened/vulnerable status, conservation-status data is currently buried in careNotes — a typed nullable string field (enum: LC/NT/VU/EN/CR/EW/EX/DD) would make conservation status machine-readable and surfaceable in the UI to inform sourcing decisions.

Orangemarked Goby is an obligate sand-sifter that uses burrows and requires a minimum sand-bed depth for burrowing and natural foraging behavior; a typed numeric field would surface this substrate depth requirement clearly in the UI, distinct from general decorPreferences. Fourth independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel), sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby), and sw-fish-502 (Black Cap Jawfish).

Fowleria isostigma is sold under at least two distinct color forms (dark cryptic tan/black vs. orange-red) as separate LiveAquaria listings; a typed colorFormNotes field would allow documenting known color variants or morphs that don't warrant separate species entries but are visually distinct enough to confuse buyers.
valueForThisSpecies: Orange-to-red body with red or yellow tail (trade name: Fowleria Cardinalfish / Redtail Cardinalfish); dark tan-and-black cryptic patterning (trade name: Speckled Fowler Cardinalfish). Relationship between forms unresolved.

reason: Deep-water species (e.g. Cave Basslets, some Assessors, Soapfishes) are consistently documented to do better under lower-intensity lighting due to their wild habitat at depth; a typed boolean would allow the UI to flag lighting-sensitive species and filter by lighting requirement.

Halichoeres marginatus (and many other wrasse species) burrow into substrate to sleep and escape perceived threats; a typed numeric field for minimum sand-bed depth would surface this substrate requirement clearly in the UI, distinct from general decorPreferences. Fifth independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel), sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby), sw-fish-502 (Black Cap Jawfish), and sw-fish-485 (Orangemarked Goby).

Interspecific hybrids (e.g. Lemonpeel x Halfblack Angelfish, Blood Parrot Cichlid) have unique husbandry implications and no single scientific name; a typed boolean would let the UI flag hybrid origin and link parent species.

Lined Dartfish and other Ptereleotris dartfish occasionally burrow but cannot excavate their own holes; burrowing/hiding dartfish and similar species (garden eels, sand gobies) need a minimum sand layer depth that cannot be expressed by decorPreferences alone — a typed numeric field would surface this substrate requirement clearly in the UI.

Flying Jet Sea Moth is sold under the trade synonym Parapegasus natans, which ichthyology currently treats as a junior synonym of Pegasus volitans (Longtail Seamoth). A typed nullable string field for the currently-accepted scientific name (distinct from the trade-name scientificName) would let the UI surface taxonomic status warnings and link synonym entries to their accepted-name counterparts without overwriting the hobby-conventional scientificName used for discovery/search.

Chinstrap Jawfish (and all jawfish) require a minimum of 7–10 cm of mixed substrate (sand + rubble) for burrow construction; a typed numeric field would surface this substrate depth requirement clearly in the UI and filter for compatible tank setups, distinct from general decorPreferences. Sixth independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel), sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby), sw-fish-502 (Black Cap Jawfish), sw-fish-485 (Orangemarked Goby), and sw-fish-511 (Dusky Wrasse).

Lori's Anthias is typically collected from 30–60 m depth; fish collected from depth may experience decompression stress during capture and often arrive in weaker condition than shallow-collected species — a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface collection-depth caution to aquarists planning acclimation and QT.

Neopomacentrus nemurus is specifically documented inhabiting silted lagoon and inshore reefs; several other coastal/estuarine fish tolerate or prefer turbid/silty conditions, and a typed boolean would allow the UI to surface turbidity-tolerance guidance and flag species less dependent on pristine, clear-water reef conditions.

Kuiter's Leopard Wrasse is a confirmed sand-burrower that sleeps buried in substrate and requires a deep, undisturbed sand bed (several inches recommended) for welfare; a typed numeric field would surface this critical tank-setup requirement in the UI, distinct from general decorPreferences. Seventh independent proposal after sw-fish-029, sw-fish-064, sw-fish-502, sw-fish-485, sw-fish-511, and sw-fish-520.

All Hippocampus seahorses (and several other aquarium trade species) are listed under CITES Appendix I or II, restricting international commercial trade; a typed nullable string field (e.g. "Appendix I", "Appendix II", null) on the top-level schema would allow the UI to surface import/export restriction warnings to aquarists and help distinguish between freely-traded and CITES-regulated species.

Caracanthus madagascariensis (and congener C. maculatus) exhibit facultative air-breathing capability documented in FishBase — a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this unusual respiratory adaptation to aquarists, relevant to tank lid design and emersion tolerance; currently buried in careNotes with no machine-readable field.

Gobiodon strangulatus (the trade name for this species) is absent from both FishBase and WoRMS public databases; the closest WoRMS accepted match is G. atrangulatus Garman, 1903. Several marine fish are sold under scientfic names that are unverified synonyms or nomen dubia; a nullable string enum field (e.g. 'accepted'/'synonym'/'nomen_dubium'/'trade_name') would allow the UI to flag taxonomic uncertainty to researchers and hobbyists without requiring the scientificName field to be nulled out.

Electric Indigo Dottyback is a captive-bred hybrid (P. fridmani x P. sankeyi); a typed boolean would let the UI flag hybrid origin and explain the hybrid scientific name notation, complementing the existing field-gap request from fw-fish-003 (Blood Parrot Cichlid).

Decorated Goby is a benthic species that inhabits coralline sand areas and likely sifts through substrate like other Istigobius/Valenciennea gobies; a typed numeric field for minimum sand depth (cm) would surface this substrate requirement clearly in the UI, distinct from general decorPreferences. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel) and sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby).

Gymnothorax prasinus is a temperate species (16–24°C) that requires active cooling in most indoor aquarium settings — a boolean flag would help the UI surface chiller-dependent species to aquarists before purchase.

Gobiodon (clown gobies) and several other marine gobies are bidirectional hermaphrodites — capable of changing sex in either direction (male-to-female or female-to-male) — which is ecologically and husbandry-relevant (any two individuals can form a mated pair) but is not captured by the existing fish.protogynous boolean (female-first only).

Sea moths (Pegasidae, including Eurypegasus draconis and Pegasus volitans) are documented to periodically shed their bony-plate skin in a single piece — likely a parasite-removal behavior — which is a medically significant husbandry trait; a typed boolean on the fish variant would allow the UI to surface this unusual behavior and advise against intervening when observed.

Oreni Tilefish is a burrowing malacanthid that excavates burrows or mounds in sandy substrate; a typed numeric field for minimum sand depth would surface this substrate requirement clearly in the UI and is distinct from general decorPreferences. Third independent proposal after sw-fish-029 (Garden Eel) and sw-fish-064 (Sleeper Blue Dot Goby).

Princess Anthias (and other deepwater-collected anthias like Lori's Anthias) are harvested from depths >30 m and require careful pressure decompression during collection — a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI surface collection-stress sensitivity and advise buyers to source from reputable collectors, distinct from general careLevel.

Cirrhilabrus rubrimarginatus is a naturally deep-water species (25–52 m), a trait shared by several fairy wrasses and deep-dwelling reef fish that informs appropriate lighting intensity and tank setup; a typed nullableNumericRange for native depth range would allow the UI to surface lower-light or dimmer-environment preferences not captured by existing fields.

Queen Purple Anthias and other high-metabolism planktivores (anthias, mandarins) require a minimum number of daily feedings for survival; a typed integer field would surface this feeding-frequency requirement in the UI distinct from feedingDifficulty and diet.notes, enabling automated reminders and filter by keeper schedule capacity.

Eviota gobies (and potentially other micro-fish genera) have scientifically documented lifespans measured in weeks to months rather than years; a typed boolean would let the UI warn aquarists that a dying fish may be natural senescence rather than disease or husbandry failure.

Pseudanthias taira is a restricted-range endemic (Northwest Pacific: Japan/Ryukyus only), while most anthias sources use broad Indo-Pacific range text; a boolean flag for restricted-range endemics would help flag species where generic care text from multi-species pages is particularly unreliable.

Foa hyalina (accepted) vs Apogonichthys hyalinus (trade synonym) — several aquarium species are listed under outdated or unaccepted scientific names by retailers; a boolean flag would let the UI warn users and display the accepted WoRMS/FishBase name alongside the trade synonym.

Trimma cana shows a notable gap between hobby-recommended temperature (LiveAquaria: 22–26°C) and FishBase wild habitat temperature (26–29°C); a typed captive-vs-wild temperature note field would let the data layer surface this discrepancy rather than burying it in careNotes.
valueForThisSpecies: Hobby sources recommend 22–26°C; FishBase wild habitat 26–29°C

reason: Red Saddled Anthias is collected from depths up to 30 m; deep-water collection depth is relevant to acclimation advice (pressure changes, lighting sensitivity) and cannot be expressed in any current schema field. A nullable number field on tank would allow this care context to be surfaced in the UI for deep-water species.

Several planktivorous anthias species are widely advised to have an attached refugium producing live copepods/amphipods to supplement frozen foods and reduce starvation risk during acclimation; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this setup recommendation to reef keepers distinct from general decorPreferences.

Ruby Red Dragonet and other Synchiropus dragonets are functionally dependent on a live copepod/amphipod population (not just 'difficult' feeders) — a typed boolean distinct from feedingDifficulty would let the UI surface requirements for a refugium and pod culture as a hard prerequisite rather than optional care tip, enabling filtering for aquarists without established pod systems.

Active planktivores like anthias require 3–5 small feedings per day rather than the standard once-daily feeding; a typed integer field would allow the UI to surface frequency-of-feeding guidance and help keepers distinguish high-metabolism species from typical carnivores/omnivores.

Randall's Anthias is found at 15–120 m depth (FishBase), with typical habitat at 15–70 m — specimens collected from deeper sites may experience barotrauma; a typed boolean would let the UI surface collection-stress sensitivity and advise sourcing from reputable collectors using proper decompression protocols. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-529 (Princess Anthias).

Several Cirrhilabrus fairy wrasses (including C. rubeus) display notably variable color intensity based on mood, stress, and reproductive state; a typed boolean would let the UI surface this trait to buyers so they understand that muted coloration in a retailer tank may not represent the species at its best.

Pseudoplesiops typus displays notable sexual dichromatism (males pinkish-red, females paler pink with yellow-green fin gradations); a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this trait so the UI can advise buyers that male/female specimens look visually different and help with sexing guidance, distinct from juvenileColorPhase (which is ontogenetic, not sex-based).

Parioglossus raoi is documented by FishBase as inhabiting marine, brackish, and freshwater environments (mangrove swamps, estuaries, inlet waters); a typed boolean on the fish variant would let the UI flag species suitable for lower-salinity or estuarine biotopes distinct from waterType=brackish. Fifth independent proposal after sw-fish-125, sw-fish-250, sw-fish-457, and sw-fish-478.

Eviota gobies have extremely short lifespans (reportedly 59 days in the wild for some Eviota species — among the shortest of any vertebrate); a documented short-lifespan advisory field or a minimum-lifespan warning would help aquarists set realistic expectations for this genus.

Trimma rubromaculatum naturally occurs at 22–50 m depth, which is deeper than typical aquarium livestock; a typed nullableNumericRange depthRange field would help surface deep-water collection origin, lighting implications (low-light preference), and acclimation notes for species sourced from mesophotic or deep reefs.

Red Spotted Dottyback is sold in the trade as Pseudochromis sp. (undescribed); a typed boolean would let the UI flag species lacking a formal scientific name, help explain why FishBase cross-verification is impossible, and surface a buyer caveat about potential reclassification.

Amblyglyphidodon ternatensis is listed as Vulnerable (VU) by IUCN (2010); a typed conservationStatus field (enum: LC/NT/VU/EN/CR/EW/EX/DD) would allow the UI to surface conservation concerns to aquarists and inform responsible sourcing decisions.

Species collected from deeper reef zones (60+ ft / 18+ m) may have decompression stress on collection and sensitivity to intense lighting; a typed boolean would help surface special acclimation guidance and light-level warnings not captured by existing fields.

Liopropoma swalesi occurs at 20–110 m depth (FishBase); this is far deeper than typical aquarium livestock and affects light acclimation requirements and collection stress — a typed nullableNumericRange depthRange field would surface deep-water origin caveats and collection ethics concerns to aquarists.

Liopropoma swalesi is reported at 20–110 m depth (FishBase), well into the mesophotic zone; a typed boolean distinct from the generic care notes would allow the UI to surface decompression-stress warnings, low-light preference cues, and responsible-sourcing concerns for deep-reef species — second independent proposal for this field after sw-fish-545.

Ostorhinchus luteus (Yellow Cardinalfish) is a paternal mouth-brooder (FishBase-confirmed); a typed boolean on the fish variant would make this reproductively significant trait machine-readable and surfaceable in breeding filter UIs — third independent proposal after sw-fish-012 (Banggai Cardinalfish) and sw-fish-031 (Gold Assessor Basslet).

Serranus basslets are synchronous hermaphrodites (functioning simultaneously as both sexes) — distinct from protogynous (female-first) or protandrous (male-first) sequential hermaphrodites; a typed boolean would let the UI surface this reproductive trait and distinguish it from the existing protogynous field.

Warthead Goby and other Paragobiodon gobies are obligate coral-dwellers that depend on live Pocillopora/Seriatopora coral colonies for food, shelter, and breeding sites; a typed boolean on the fish variant would surface this habitat-dependency warning distinct from decorPreferences and alert aquarists that live stony coral hosts are required for long-term health.

Tracey's Damselfish juveniles display vibrant yellow coloration with blue markings, distinctly different from the adult purple-to-yellow sunset pattern; a typed boolean would allow the UI to warn buyers that the fish may look very different when mature.

Lythrypnus zebra and similar temperate-zone marine fish (Catalina Goby, some blennies) require significantly cooler water than typical tropical reef tanks; a boolean flag would let the UI warn aquarists about temperature incompatibility with standard reef setups.

Blastomussa and many other LPS corals are routinely propagated by fragging (cutting between individual corallites) in captivity; a typed boolean on the coral variant would surface hobbyist-propagation suitability and is a commonly sought trait in the reef community. Second independent proposal for this field after sw-coral-001 (Acropora).

Blastomussa and other LPS corals tolerate moderate nutrient levels; care sources consistently specify a target nitrate range (5–10 ppm for Blastomussa specifically per ReefChasers); a typed nullableNumericRange for nitrate would make reef water-quality guidance machine-readable rather than buried in careNotes. Fourth independent proposal for this field after sw-coral-002 (Duncanopsammia), sw-coral-001 (Acropora), and sw-coral-003 (Goniopora).

Bubble Tip Anemones (and some other anemone species) actively roam the aquarium and will contact unguarded powerhead impellers, causing shredding, tank fouling, and potential wipeout; a typed boolean on the coral variant would surface a safety warning for this well-known reef-keeping hazard distinct from aggression range or placement.

Xenia and some other soft corals (Kenya Tree, GSP) spread aggressively by runners and encrustation in ways that differ fundamentally from the stinging-aggression captured by aggressionRangeCm; a typed enum (slow/moderate/rapid) on the coral variant would let the UI warn keepers about tank-takeover risk and propagation potential separately from chemical/physical aggression.

Millepora fire coral (and other nematocyst-bearing organisms like anemones and some zoanthids) pose genuine handler-safety hazards distinct from fish venomousSpines; a typed enum (e.g. nematocyst/venomous/toxic/none) would allow the UI to surface PPE warnings on the species detail page.

Capnella and similar fast-spreading soft corals (Xenia, Clove Polyp) regularly drop branches or buds that reattach autonomously; a typed boolean would let the UI warn reef keepers about invasive spread risk and distinguish self-fragmenting corals from those that only respond to intentional fragging.

Gorgonia ventalina and all Hippocampus seahorses are CITES Appendix II; a typed nullable string field (e.g. "Appendix I", "Appendix II", null) on the top-level schema would let the UI surface import/export restriction warnings to aquarists and distinguish between freely-traded and CITES-regulated species. Second independent proposal after sw-fish-516 (Half-Spined Seahorse).
